senses.definition: the act of loading something beforehand or prior to use
examples: The software includes preloads to speed up startup.
They examined the preloads of data before the test run.

senses.definition: to load something before it is needed or before a process begins
examples: The installer preloads components to speed up installation.
They preloads the page with scripts to improve performance.

senses.definition: an amount of money or funds provided before a main loan, often as an advance or preliminary loan
examples: The bank offered a preloan to cover startup costs.
A preloan was used to secure the larger mortgage.

senses.definition: the process of preparing content to be localized before translation, typically adapting for regional uses
examples: Prelocalization helps ensure smooth localization later.
Teams performed prelocalization checks before translation projects.

senses.definition: to place or position something in advance at or near a location for future access
examples: They prelocate equipment near the site for quicker deployment.
Officials prelocate supplies to the border ahead of a forecasted influx.

senses.definition: placed or positioned in advance at a location
examples: The prelocated crates were ready for quick pickup.
Prelocated items reduced response time.

senses.definition: third-person singular of prelocate; places or positions something in advance
examples: The team prelocates gear for tomorrow's operation.
It prelocates documents to the secure vault.

senses.definition: the act of placing something in advance at a location
examples: They are prelocating supplies ahead of the event.
Prelocating assets helps with rapid response.

senses.definition: a stage before logical reasoning; rudimentary or intuitive thinking
examples: The theory explored prelogic patterns in language.
Students study prelogic concepts early in philosophy courses.

senses.definition: relating to or denoting a stage prior to formal logic
examples: Prelogic ideas shaped early hypotheses.
A prelogic framework guided the study.

senses.definition: not yet applying formal logic; based on intuition or approximate reasoning
examples: Prelogical judgments can be biased.
Children often rely on prelogical thinking before formal schooling.

senses.definition: relating to a position in front of or before the lips or mouth area
examples: Preloral gestures accompanied the speech.
Medical notes described preloral anatomy.

senses.definition: relating to or occurring before the lore or myth of a culture; pre-lore or ancestral knowledge
examples: Preloreal beliefs influenced early rituals.
Researchers studied preloreal traditions.

senses.definition: the anticipated loss of something before it occurs; a premonition of losing
examples: He feared preloss of the data if the system failed.
Preloss considerations shaped their backup plan.

senses.definition: second-hand but cherished; previously owned and now loved again
examples: She bought preloved books from the charity shop.
preloved items can be in good condition at a discount.

senses.definition: an introduction or opening piece that leads into something more important
examples: The symphony begins with a lively prelude.
The conference opened with a short prelude of remarks.

senses.definition: to introduce or begin with a prelude
examples: The author preludes the article with a personal anecdote.
The speech preluded with a brief story.

senses.definition: past tense of prelude; introduced or began with an opening piece or opening remarks
examples: The performance preluded with a piano solo.
He preluded the report with a short summary.

senses.definition: one who preludes; something that serves as an introduction
examples: The overture acts as a preluder to the opera.
It served as a preluder to the main discussion.

senses.definition: plural of preluder; individuals or items that serve as introductions
examples: The preluders set the tone for the evening.
Several preluders were performed before the main act.

senses.definition: short pieces of music that introduce a larger work; introductory sections
examples: Bach's preludes are familiar to many pianists.
The piece contains several preludes before the fugue.

senses.definition: plural of preludio; a musical prelude or introductory piece in certain languages
examples: They studied several preludi as part of the baroque suite.
The preludi set the mood for the night.

senses.definition: relating to or resembling a prelude; introductory
examples: The preludial section set the atmosphere.
A preludial motif appears early in the piece.

senses.definition: present participle of prelude; performing or acting as an introduction
examples: The concert was preluding with a soft piano piece.
She is preluding the talk with a short anecdote.

senses.definition: a musical prelude; an introductory movement or section in some languages (e.g., Italian)
examples: The preludio set the tempo for the aria.
They played the preludio before the cantata.

senses.definition: characterized by or relating to preludes; introductory
examples: The preludious sections were short and atmospheric.
A preludious mood filled the hall.

senses.definition: in a preludious, introductory manner
examples: The narration moved preludiously into the main tale.
She spoke preludiously to set the scene.

senses.definition: an introductory piece of music; a short prelude
examples: The organist played a preludium before the hymn.
Chamber music often begins with a preludium.
